content:
# Romans Chapter 12 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Romans Chapter 12 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 12:1 — "spiritual worship" vs. "reasonable worship" vs. "truly the way to worship him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to present the bodies of you [as] a sacrifice — living, holy, well-pleasing to the God — the reasonable worship of you." | "Reasonable worship" for λογικὴν λατρείαν; literal syntax |
| LOGOS | "to present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God, which is your spiritual worship." | "Spiritual worship" for λογικὴν λατρείαν |
| PNEUMA | "I plead with you to give your bodies to God because of all he has done for you. Let them be a living and holy sacrifice — the kind he will find acceptable. This is truly the way to worship him." | "Truly the way to worship him" — paraphrase; "plead" for παρακαλῶ; adds "because of all he has done" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. λογικὴν λατρείαν is one of the most debated phrases in Romans — "reasonable" (EMET) captures the λόγος connection, "spiritual" (LOGOS) is the traditional rendering, and PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the term entirely. "Spiritual worship" is retained as the consensus choice, with the range of meaning discussed in notes.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Spiritual worship" retained with note on the range of λογικός.

---

## 12:1 — "I appeal" vs. "I urge" vs. "I plead"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I urge you therefore" | "Urge" for παρακαλῶ |
| LOGOS | "I appeal to you therefore" | "Appeal" for παρακαλῶ |
| PNEUMA | "I plead with you" | "Plead" for παρακαλῶ — stronger emotional register |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "appeal." παρακαλῶ covers a range from "urge" to "exhort" to "comfort." "Appeal" captures the pastoral authority without PNEUMA's emotional intensity ("plead") or EMET's directness ("urge").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:2 — "conformed to this world" vs. "conformed to this age" vs. "copy the behavior and customs of this world"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "do not be conformed to this age" | "Age" for αἰών — more precise |
| LOGOS | "Do not be conformed to this world" | "World" for αἰών — standard English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "Don't copy the behavior and customs of this world, but let God transform you into a new person by changing the way you think." | Full paraphrase; "copy" for συσχηματίζεσθε; adds "customs," "new person," "the way you think" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "age" (αἰών) is technically more accurate than "world" (which would be κόσμος), but "this world" is the standard English rendering and communicates effectively. PNEUMA's expansion loses the compact imperative force of the Greek and adds multiple concepts not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "World" used with note that αἰών means "age."

---

## 12:2 — "perfect" vs. "complete" — τέλειον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the good and well-pleasing and complete" | "Complete" for τέλειον |
| LOGOS | "what is good and acceptable and perfect" | "Perfect" for τέλειον |
| PNEUMA | "which is good and pleasing and perfect" | "Perfect" — agrees with LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "perfect." Both "perfect" and "complete" are valid for τέλειον. "Perfect" is the traditional rendering and forms a stronger climactic sequence: good → acceptable → perfect.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 12:3 — "sober judgment" vs. "think soberly" vs. "be honest in your evaluation"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to think so as to think soberly, as the God distributed to each a measure of faith" | Preserves the Greek wordplay on φρονεῖν (four uses of the root) |
| LOGOS | "to think with sober judgment, each according to the measure of faith that God has assigned" | "Sober judgment" — noun phrase |
| PNEUMA | "Be honest in your evaluation of yourselves, measuring yourselves by the faith God has given us." | "Honest evaluation" — paraphrase; "given us" changes from individual to collective |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The verse contains a remarkable wordplay in Greek (φρονεῖν... ὑπερφρονεῖν... φρονεῖν... σωφρονεῖν) that no English translation can fully capture. LOGOS's "sober judgment" captures the σωφρονεῖν adequately. PNEUMA's "honest evaluation" loses the thinking/mindset vocabulary.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:8 — "with zeal" vs. "in diligence" vs. "take the responsibility seriously"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] leading, in diligence" | "Diligence" for σπουδῇ |
| LOGOS | "the one who leads, with zeal" | "Zeal" for σπουδῇ |
| PNEUMA | "If God has given you leadership ability, take the responsibility seriously." | Full paraphrase; "take the responsibility seriously" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "with zeal." σπουδή means "eagerness, diligence, earnestness." "Zeal" captures the active energy better than EMET's more restrained "diligence." PNEUMA's paraphrase eliminates the concise participial list structure Paul uses.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:9 — "genuine" vs. "unhypocritical" vs. "Don't just pretend to love others"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The love [be] unhypocritical." | "Unhypocritical" for ἀνυπόκριτος — etymological |
| LOGOS | "Let love be genuine." | "Genuine" for ἀνυπόκριτος |
| PNEUMA | "Don't just pretend to love others. Really love them." | Two sentences; paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "unhypocritical" is etymologically precise (ἀν-υπόκριτος = "without-mask") but clumsy in English. LOGOS's "genuine" is idiomatic and accurate. PNEUMA's paraphrase is effective but loses the compact, imperative quality of the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:10 — "Outdo one another in showing honor" vs. "leading one another" in honor

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in the honor, leading one another" | "Leading" for προηγούμενοι — interpreting as "going before" |
| LOGOS | "Outdo one another in showing honor." | "Outdo" — interprets προηγούμενοι as competitive generosity |
| PNEUMA | "take delight in honoring each other." | "Take delight" — emotional rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The participle προηγούμενοι can mean "leading the way" or "outdoing." LOGOS's "outdo one another" captures the competitive generosity Paul envisions — a race to show honor rather than to receive it. PNEUMA's "take delight" is weaker.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:11 — "fervent in spirit" vs. "serve the Lord enthusiastically"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in the Spirit, fervent; serving the Lord" | Capitalizes "Spirit" — interpreting πνεύματι as Holy Spirit |
| LOGOS | "be fervent in spirit, serve the Lord" | Lowercase "spirit" — human spirit or disposition |
| PNEUMA | "work hard and serve the Lord enthusiastically" | Paraphrases both clauses; "enthusiastically" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering with lowercase "spirit." The ambiguity of πνεύματι (Spirit or spirit) is a genuine interpretive question. LOGOS wisely preserves the ambiguity with lowercase "spirit." PNEUMA's "enthusiastically" collapses "fervent in spirit" into a single adverb.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Lowercase "spirit" preserves ambiguity.

---

## 12:13 — "seek to show hospitality" vs. "pursuing the hospitality" vs. "always be eager to practice hospitality"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "pursuing the hospitality" | "Pursuing" for διώκοντες — strong verb (same word used for "persecute") |
| LOGOS | "seek to show hospitality" | "Seek to show" — softer |
| PNEUMA | "Always be eager to practice hospitality." | Adds "always"; "eager to practice" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering, though EMET's "pursuing" better captures the forceful verb διώκω (literally "chase, pursue, persecute"). The same verb that Paul uses for persecuting the church (Galatians 1:13) he now uses for pursuing hospitality — a deliberate irony. This nuance is noted in SOPHIA's commentary.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS, with note on the force of διώκω.

---

## 12:16 — "associate with the lowly" vs. "being led along with the lowly" vs. "enjoy the company of ordinary people"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not thinking the high [things], but being led along with the lowly" | Ambiguous — "lowly" could be neuter (humble tasks) or masculine (humble people) |
| LOGOS | "Do not be haughty, but associate with the lowly." | "Associate with the lowly" — takes masculine reading |
| PNEUMA | "Don't be too proud to enjoy the company of ordinary people. And don't think you know it all!" | "Ordinary people" — interpretive; adds "don't think you know it all" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The Greek ταπεινοῖς could be masculine ("the lowly/humble people") or neuter ("lowly things/humble tasks"). Both readings are valid. LOGOS takes the personal reading which fits the context of community relationships. PNEUMA's "ordinary people" is too casual and adds "And don't think you know it all" which is a separate clause in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:19 — "leave it to the wrath of God" vs. "give place to the wrath" vs. "leave that to the righteous anger of God"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "give place to the wrath" | Literal; does not specify whose wrath |
| LOGOS | "leave it to the wrath of God" | Identifies "the wrath" as God's |
| PNEUMA | "Leave that to the righteous anger of God." | "Righteous anger" — adds qualifier; conversational register |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "give place to the wrath" preserves the Greek ambiguity (τῇ ὀργῇ — "the wrath" without possessive) but is unclear in English. The Deuteronomy quotation that follows ("Vengeance is mine") makes clear this is God's wrath. PNEUMA's addition of "righteous" is interpretive.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 12:20 — "heap burning coals on his head" — interpretation

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you will heap coals of fire upon the head of him" | Literal; "coals of fire" |
| LOGOS | "by so doing you will heap burning coals on his head" | "Burning coals" — standard English |
| PNEUMA | "In doing this, you will heap burning coals of shame on their heads." | Adds "of shame" — interpretive addition |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA adds "of shame" which is one interpretation of the burning coals metaphor (causing shame/repentance) but is not in the Greek text. The metaphor should remain uninterpreted in the translation, with discussion in notes.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. No interpretive gloss added to the metaphor.

---

## 12:21 — "overcome evil with good" vs. "conquer the evil by the good" vs. "conquer evil by doing good"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Do not be conquered by the evil, but conquer the evil by the good." | "Conquered/conquer" — preserves the military metaphor of νικάω |
| LOGOS | "Do not be overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good." | "Overcome" — widely recognized rendering |
| PNEUMA | "Don't let evil conquer you, but conquer evil by doing good." | "Doing good" — adds verbal form |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Overcome" is the most recognized rendering of this verse. EMET's "conquered/conquer" preserves the νικάω root well. PNEUMA's "by doing good" changes the abstract noun ("good") to a verbal phrase.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| λογικὴν λατρείαν: three different renderings | v.1 | "Reasonable worship" / "spiritual worship" / paraphrase |
| PNEUMA breaks compact imperatives into conversational sentences | vv.1, 3, 8, 9, 13 | Expanding participial lists into full clauses |
| EMET's hyper-literal possessives and articles | vv.1, 3, 19, 20 | "The bodies of you," "the heart of me," "the head of him" |
| Theological vocabulary precision | vv.2, 9, 11 | αἰών, ἀνυπόκριτος, πνεύματι |
| Interpretive additions by PNEUMA | vv.1, 16, 19, 20 | "Because of all he has done," "ordinary people," "of shame" |
| Strong verb selection (διώκω, νικάω) | vv.13, 21 | "Pursuing" / "seek" / "eager"; "conquer" / "overcome" |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Romans 12 marks the ethical turn of the letter, and the translation challenges shift accordingly — from theological density to imperative compression. Paul uses rapid-fire participial clauses (especially vv.9-21) that function as a virtue catalogue. EMET preserves the participial structure but produces choppy English. LOGOS converts participles into smooth imperatives. PNEUMA expands each imperative into a full conversational sentence, which aids comprehension but destroys the staccato rhythm that is part of Paul's rhetorical effect. The most significant theological divergence is on λογικὴν λατρείαν (v.1), where the three renderings ("reasonable," "spiritual," "truly the way to worship") each capture a different facet of the Greek. SOPHIA chose "spiritual worship" as the consensus, acknowledging that no single English word encompasses the full semantic range of λογικός.

---
